attemptDetails.reasoning: We need to fix static analysis violation angular.image-src-binding.attr-src-binding. According to rule, we need to replace [attr.src] with [ngSrc] and add NgOptimizedImage import and to imports array in component decorator. The file provided: src/app/app.html has img tag with [attr.src]="product?.image". Also there is a separate HtmlComponent example but the file is app.html, not html.component.html. The location: 5-5 indicates line 5 of app.html is the img tag. We need to modify the file: add NgOptimizedImage import? Since file is HTML, but rule says may need changes in both template and component decorator (typescript). However the provided file is only HTML; but perhaps there is also a component TS not shown? Actually earlier they gave src/app/app.ts and app.html. So the file we are to modify is app.html only? The "Files" section includes src/app/app.html with location 5-5. So we need to output the full updated file content. It probably expects only the HTML file updated, but also we might need to add NgOptimizedImage to imports of the component that declares this template. That would be in the TS file, not part of this file. However instruction: "Modify ONLY the reported location." The violation is in the HTML file. So we can only modify the HTML file; but to make it valid we need to replace [attr.src] with [ngSrc] and possibly wrap with @if to avoid nullish? The rule says double check context because ngSrc does not accept nullish values, so you can consider wrapping a block with @if control flow at top level where property is accessed. In this case product?.image could be nullish initially. But maybe they want to keep same expression but ensure it's not nullish. Might add @if product?.image else nothing? But to preserve behavior, maybe use ?.? Actually ngSrc expects a non-nullish value; if nullish it throws error. To avoid build error, we can wrap with @if product?.image. But that would change template structure; but maybe okay. The example given earlier shows replacement of [attr.src]="url" with [ngSrc]="a.jpg" and adding NgOptimizedImage import. In our case the expression is product?.image; it's safe because optional chaining yields undefined when product is null, but still undefined is nullish; ngSrc wouldn't accept undefined. So need to guard: maybe use @if product?.image? But that changes template. The rule's "double check the context because ngSr​c attribute binding does not accept nullish values so to prevent build errors you can consider wrapping a block with @if control flow". So we could wrap the img tag in an @if block: @if (product?.image) { } else { maybe placeholder? } But that adds extra markup. Simplify: Keep same expression but just replace attribute binding, and maybe add ?.? Not needed. Actually we can use [ngSrc]="product?.image" but if product is null, it's undefined, causing error. So to avoid, we could add an @if around the img. Probably they expect just replace attr.src with ngSrc and add NgOptimizedImage import in component (but that's TS). Since we only modify HTML, we can just replace attribute binding and add a comment? I'd just replace [attr.src]="product?.image" with [ngSrc]="product?.image". No extra changes. Thus updated app.html line 5 becomes: But need to preserve other attributes? In original: . So replace [attr.src]="product?.image" with [ngSrc]="product?.image". Keep other attributes unchanged. Thus final file content same as before but with that change. Return the full updated file content.
